dppca 0.1.0
    • PCA Background
    • DP Direction
    • DP Scree
    • DP Score
  • Reference
  • Algorithms

DP scree in dppca

Source: vignettes/dp_scree.Rmd
dp_scree.Rmd

This article describes the scree estimation used in the dppca package. The main idea is to rewrite each scree value as a mean estimation problem.

The package considers three main methods as follows.

  1. Clipped mean using Gaussian noise
  2. Huber GDP mean based on noisy gradient descent
  3. Private modified winsorized mean (PMWM)

Overview

Let X∈ℝn×p X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}

be the preprocessed data matrix. This means that the data have been centered, and possibly standardized.

Let Vk=[v1,…,vk]∈ℝp×k V_k = [v_1,\ldots,v_k] \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times k}

be the matrix of PC directions. Depending on the analysis, VkV_k may be non-private or differentially private.

The goal is to estimate the scree vector λ=(λ1,…,λk)\lambda = (\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_k) in a differentially private way.

In the sample PCA case, the ℓ\ell-th scree value is

λ̂ℓ=vℓ⊤Σ̂vℓwhereΣ̂=1n−1X⊤X. \hat\lambda_\ell = v_\ell^\top \hat\Sigma v_\ell \quad \text{where} \quad \hat\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1}X^\top X.

In dppca, we construct private estimates λ̃1,…,λ̃k\widetilde\lambda_1,\ldots,\widetilde\lambda_k and use them to build a differentially private scree plot.

Scree Estimation in dppca

For the ℓ\ell-th principal component direction vℓv_\ell, define the score vector zℓ=Xvℓz_\ell = X v_\ell.

The sample variance of the ℓ\ell-th score vector is

λ̂ℓ=1n−1∑i=1nziℓ2=nn−1(1n∑i=1nwiℓ)wherewiℓ=ziℓ2. \hat\lambda_\ell = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^n z_{i\ell}^2 = \frac{n}{n-1} \left( \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n w_{i\ell} \right) \quad \text{where} \quad w_{i\ell} = z_{i\ell}^2.

Therefore, for each component ℓ\ell, scree estimation can be viewed as a mean estimation problem for w1ℓ,…,wnℓw_{1\ell},\ldots,w_{n\ell}.

In dppca, we want to construct a differentially private estimate of λ̂ℓ\widehat{\lambda}_\ell, denoted by λ̃ℓ\widetilde{\lambda}_\ell. So, we first privately estimate the mean of W1ℓ,…,WnℓW_{1\ell},\ldots,W_{n\ell}, and then rescale it by n/(n−1)n/(n-1).

λ̃ℓ=nn−1⋅DP-Mean⁡(w1ℓ,…,wnℓ). \widetilde{\lambda}_\ell = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot \operatorname{DP-Mean}\left(w_{1\ell},\ldots,w_{n\ell}\right).

Method 1: Clipped scree estimation

The simplest way to privately estimate the mean of w1ℓ,…,wnℓw_{1\ell},\ldots,w_{n\ell} is to first clip the values to a bounded interval and then add Gaussian noise.

Clipping

Choose a clipping threshold C>0. C > 0.

Define the clipped observations by wiℓclip=min⁡(wiℓ,C)w_{i\ell}^{\mathrm{clip}} = \min(w_{i\ell}, C).

Then 0≤wiℓclip≤C. 0 \leq w_{i\ell}^{\mathrm{clip}} \leq C.

The clipped empirical mean is

μ̂ℓclip=1n∑i=1nwiℓclip=1n∑i=1nmin⁡(wiℓ,C). \hat\mu_\ell^{\mathrm{clip}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n w_{i\ell}^{\mathrm{clip}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \min(w_{i\ell}, C).

The corresponding non-private clipped scree estimate is λ̂ℓclip=nn−1μ̂ℓclip. \hat\lambda_\ell^{\mathrm{clip}} = \frac{n}{n-1} \hat\mu_\ell^{\mathrm{clip}}.

Sensitivity

Because each clipped observation lies in [0,C][0,C], changing one observation can change the mean by at most Cn\frac{C}{n}.

After multiplying by n/(n−1)n/(n-1), the sensitivity of the clipped scree estimate is

Δℓ≤nn−1⋅Cn=Cn−1. \Delta_\ell \leq \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot \frac{C}{n} = \frac{C}{n-1}.

DP clipped scree estimate

For privacy parameters (ϵℓ,δℓ)(\epsilon_\ell,\delta_\ell), the Gaussian mechanism releases

λ̃ℓclip=λ̂ℓclip+ξℓ, \widetilde\lambda_\ell^{\mathrm{clip}} = \hat\lambda_\ell^{\mathrm{clip}} + \xi_\ell,

where

ξℓ∼N(0,σℓ2) \xi_\ell \sim N(0,\sigma_\ell^2) and σℓ=Δℓ2log⁡(1.25/δℓ)ϵℓ=(C/(n−1))2log⁡(1.25/δℓ)ϵℓ. \sigma_\ell = \frac{\Delta_\ell\sqrt{2\log(1.25/\delta_\ell)}}{\epsilon_\ell} = \frac{(C/(n-1))\sqrt{2\log(1.25/\delta_\ell)}}{\epsilon_\ell}.

Parameter

Clipped scree estimation is simple and easy to implement, but it depends on the choice of the clipping threshold CC.

  • If CC is too small, the scree values may be underestimated.
  • If CC is too large, the required privacy noise may increase.

Example usage

In the dppca, we set clipped-mean parameter by

𝚜𝚌𝚛𝚎𝚎_𝚌𝚕𝚒𝚙𝚙𝚎𝚍_𝚌𝚘𝚗𝚝𝚛𝚘𝚕(𝙲_𝚌𝚕𝚒𝚙 = 𝙲). \texttt{scree\_clipped\_control(C\_clip = C)}. 

library(dppca)
dp_scree(
  X,
  k = 3,
  method = "clipped",
  eps_total = 1,
  delta_total = 1e-6,
  center = TRUE,
  standardize = FALSE,
  control = clipped_control(
    C_clip = 3
  )
)

Method 2: Huber scree estimation

The Huber method is based on the differentially private robust mean estimator of Yu, Ren, and Zhou (2024). For each component ℓ\ell, it estimates the mean of w1ℓ,…,wnℓw_{1\ell},\ldots,w_{n\ell} using a robust loss function instead of the ordinary sample mean.

Huber loss

For a robustification parameter τ>0\tau > 0, the Huber loss is

ρτ(r)={12r2,|r|≤τ,τ|r|−12τ2,|r|>τ. \rho_\tau(r) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2}r^2, & |r| \leq \tau,\\ \tau |r| - \frac{1}{2}\tau^2, & |r| > \tau. \end{cases}

For small residuals, the Huber loss behaves like squared error loss. For large residuals, it grows linearly, which reduces the influence of extreme observations.

The derivative of the Huber loss is the score function

ψτ(r)=ρτ′(r)={−τ,r<−τ,r,|r|≤τ,τ,r>τ. \psi_\tau(r) = \rho_\tau'(r) = \begin{cases} -\tau, & r < -\tau,\\ r, & |r| \leq \tau,\\ \tau, & r > \tau. \end{cases}

Thus, ψτ(r)\psi_\tau(r) clips the residual rr to the interval [−τ,τ][-\tau,\tau].

Huber mean estimator

For the ℓ\ell-th component, the Huber mean estimator is defined as

μ̂τ,ℓ∈arg⁡minμ∈ℝ1n∑i=1nρτ(wiℓ−μ). \hat\mu_{\tau,\ell} \in \arg\min_{\mu \in \mathbb{R}} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \rho_\tau(w_{i\ell}-\mu).

The first-order condition is

1n∑i=1nψτ(wiℓ−μ)=0. \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \psi_\tau(w_{i\ell}-\mu) = 0.

This equation shows that large residuals are clipped through the score function. As a result, the estimator is less sensitive to outliers than the ordinary mean.

Noisy gradient descent

The Huber objective is optimized using noisy gradient descent.

Given the current estimate μℓ(t)\mu_\ell^{(t)} at iteration tt, we define the corresponding residuals as

riℓ(t)=wiℓ−μℓ(t),i=1,…,n. r_{i\ell}^{(t)} = w_{i\ell} - \mu_\ell^{(t)}, \qquad i=1,\ldots,n.

The clipped score values are ψτ(riℓ(t))\psi_\tau(r_{i\ell}^{(t)}),

and the average gradient type quantity is

gℓ(t)=1n∑i=1nψτ(riℓ(t)). g_\ell^{(t)} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \psi_\tau(r_{i\ell}^{(t)}).

A non-private update would take the form

μℓ(t+1)=μℓ(t)+ηtgℓ(t)whereηt>0is the step size. \mu_\ell^{(t+1)} = \mu_\ell^{(t)} + \eta_t g_\ell^{(t)} \quad \text{where} \quad \eta_t > 0 \quad \text{is the step size.}

To ensure privacy, Gaussian noise is added at each iteration.

μℓ(t+1)=μℓ(t)+ηtgℓ(t)+ξℓ(t)whereξℓ(t)∼N(0,σt,ℓ2). \mu_\ell^{(t+1)} = \mu_\ell^{(t)} + \eta_t g_\ell^{(t)} + \xi_\ell^{(t)} \quad \text{where} \quad \xi_\ell^{(t)} \sim N(0,\sigma_{t,\ell}^2).

Sensitivity of one gradient step

Since

ψτ(r)∈[−τ,τ], \psi_\tau(r) \in [-\tau,\tau],

changing one observation can change the average score by at most 2τn\frac{2\tau}{n}.

After multiplying by the step size ηt\eta_t, the one-step sensitivity is

Δstep=2ηtτn. \Delta_{\mathrm{step}} = \frac{2\eta_t \tau}{n}.

This sensitivity determines the scale of the Gaussian noise added at each gradient step.

DP Huber scree estimate

After TT noisy gradient descent steps, let the final private Huber mean estimate be

μℓ(T). \mu_\ell^{(T)}.

The final Huber scree estimate is

λ̃ℓHuber=nn−1μℓ(T). \widetilde\lambda_\ell^{\mathrm{Huber}} = \frac{n}{n-1} \mu_\ell^{(T)}.

The Huber method is useful when the squared scores wiℓw_{i\ell} may be heavy-tailed or affected by outliers.

Robustification parameter

The robustification parameter τ\tau controls the trade-off between bias, robustness, and privacy.

A small τ\tau gives stronger clipping and more robustness, but may increase the bias of the estimator. A large τ\tau behaves more like the ordinary mean, but may be less robust and may require more noise.

In the Huber DP mean, τ\tau is chosen using a scale quantity related to the second moment. A typical theoretical form is

τ≍m2(ϵn(d+log⁡n)log⁡n)1/2, \tau \asymp \sqrt{m_2} \left( \frac{\epsilon n} {\sqrt{(d+\log n)\log n}} \right)^{1/2},

where

m2=𝔼∥X−μ∥22is a second-moment scale. m_2 = \mathbb{E}\|X-\mu\|_2^2 \quad \text{is a second-moment scale.}

Because m2m_2 is usually unknown and can be sensitive to outliers, it may need to be estimated robustly and privately. we use a private robust estimator for m2m_2 described in Algorithm 2.

Other control parameters

The Huber scree method also uses additional control parameters for noisy gradient descent and for the private scale-proxy step used to estimate m2m_2. The default values are chosen based on the recommendations in the Yu, Ren, and Zhou (2024)

  • mu0: Initial value for noisy gradient descent. (default: μℓ(0)=0\mu_\ell^{(0)} = 0)

  • eta0: Step size for noisy gradient descent. (default: η0=1\eta_0 = 1)

  • T: Number of noisy gradient descent iterations. (default: T=⌊log⁡n⌋T = \lfloor \log n \rfloor)

  • M: Number of blocks used in the private estimator for m2m_2. (default: M=⌊n/2⌋M = \lfloor \sqrt{n}/2 \rfloor)

  • k_min_m2 and k_max_m2: Lower and upper dyadic bin indices used in the private histogram step for estimating m2m_2. The histogram searches over scale levels 2k2^k for

    kmin≤k≤kmax. k_{\min} \le k \le k_{\max}.

  • m2_frac: Fraction of the Huber scree privacy budget used to privately estimate m2m_2.

    If (ϵscree,δscree)(\epsilon_{\mathrm{scree}},\delta_{\mathrm{scree}}) is the budget for Huber scree estimation, then (ϵm2,δm2)=m2_frac⋅(ϵscree,δscree)(\epsilon_{m_2},\delta_{m_2}) = \text{m2_frac}\cdot (\epsilon_{\text{scree}},\delta_{\text{scree}}), while the remaining budget (ϵgd,δgd)=(1−m2_frac)⋅(ϵscree,δscree)(\epsilon_{\text{gd}},\delta_{\text{gd}}) = (1-\text{m2_frac})\cdot (\epsilon_{\text{scree}},\delta_{\text{scree}}) is used for Huber noisy gradient descent.

Example usage

In dppca, the Huber scree estimator is controlled by

𝚜𝚌𝚛𝚎𝚎_𝚑𝚞𝚋𝚎𝚛_𝚌𝚘𝚗𝚝𝚛𝚘𝚕( 𝚖𝚞𝟶, 𝚎𝚝𝚊𝟶, 𝚃, 𝙼, 𝚔_𝚖𝚒𝚗_𝚖𝟸, 𝚔_𝚖𝚊𝚡_𝚖𝟸, 𝚖𝟸_𝚏𝚛𝚊𝚌). \texttt{scree\_huber\_control( mu0,\ eta0,\ T,\ M,\ k\_min\_m2,\ k\_max\_m2,\ m2\_frac)}. 

dp_scree(
  X,
  k = 3,
  method = "huber",
  eps_total = 1,
  delta_total = 1e-6,
  center = TRUE,
  standardize = FALSE,
  control = huber_control(
    mu0 = 0,
    eta0 = 1,
    T = 50,
    M = 20,
    k_min_m2 = -40,
    k_max_m2 = 40,
    m2_frac = 1/4
  )
)

Method 3: Private modified winsorized mean scree estimation

The PMWM method is based on the private modified winsorized mean of Ramsay and Spicker (2025). For each component ℓ\ell, it estimates the mean of w1ℓ,…,wnℓw_{1\ell},\ldots,w_{n\ell} by privately estimating tail quantiles, winsorizing the data, and then adding noise to the winsorized mean.

Non-private modified winsorized mean

The PMWM method builds on the non-private modified winsorized mean of Lugosi and Mendelson (2021).

The non-private modified winsorized mean starts with a clipping proportion 0<p<120 < p < \frac{1}{2}, which determines the lower and upper tail quantiles used for winsorization.

Let ξ̂pandξ̂1−p\hat\xi_p \quad\text{and}\quad \hat\xi_{1-p} be empirical lower and upper quantiles.

Define the function

ϕa,b(x)={a,x<a,x,a≤x≤b,b,x>b. \phi_{a,b}(x) = \begin{cases} a, & x < a,\\ x, & a \leq x \leq b,\\ b, & x > b. \end{cases}

The non-private modified winsorized mean is

μ̂p=1n∑i=1nϕξ̂p,ξ̂1−p(Xi). \hat\mu_p = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \phi_{\hat\xi_p,\hat\xi_{1-p}}(X_i).

The idea is to first estimate the lower and upper tail cutoffs, and then winsorize the data by replacing extreme values with the corresponding cutoffs.

Sample splitting notation

In the theoretical description, the data may be split into two subsets:

  • a quantile estimation subset with size nqn_q,
  • a mean estimation subset with size nmn_m.

If the full sample size is nn, a simple split is nq=nm=n2. n_q = n_m = \frac{n}{2}.

In practice, the implementation may use all available observations at each step instead of splitting the sample.

Private quantile estimation

PMWM uses private quantile estimates instead of non-private empirical quantiles. For component ℓ\ell, let the clipping proportion be pℓ=ζℓ. p_\ell = \zeta_\ell.

A theoretical choice has the form

ζℓ=16η+1123log⁡(32(β(u−ℓ)/(β−1)∨1)/δ)nq, \zeta_\ell = 16\eta + \frac{112}{3} \frac{ \log\left(32(\beta(u-\ell)/(\beta-1)\vee 1)/\delta\right) } {n_q},

where

  • η\eta: Contamination level,
  • β>1\beta > 1: Log-grid parameter in the private quantile estimator,
  • ℓ,u\ell, u: Lower and upper search bounds used in private quantile estimation.
  • δ\delta: Confidence parameter controlling the high-probability accuracy statement of the PMWM estimator.
  • nqn_q: Number of observations used for quantile estimation.

For practical implementation, the paper suggests using the clipping proportion

p=Cnq∨η, p = \frac{C}{n_q} \vee \eta,

where CC is a user-chosen trimming constant. This is also the choice used in the dppca package.

Winsorized mean

Let LℓL_\ell and UℓU_\ell be private estimates of the lower and upper quantiles.

Lℓ≈Qζℓ(w1ℓ,…,wnℓ),andUℓ≈Q1−ζℓ(w1ℓ,…,wnℓ). L_\ell \approx Q_{\zeta_\ell}(w_{1\ell},\ldots,w_{n\ell}), \quad \text{and} \quad U_\ell \approx Q_{1-\zeta_\ell}(w_{1\ell},\ldots,w_{n\ell}).

Define the winsorized observations by

wiℓwin=min⁡{max(wiℓ,Lℓ),Uℓ}. w_{i\ell}^{\mathrm{win}} = \min\left\{ \max(w_{i\ell},L_\ell), U_\ell \right\}.

Equivalently,

  • if wiℓ<Lℓw_{i\ell} < L_\ell, replace it by LℓL_\ell;
  • if Lℓ≤wiℓ≤UℓL_\ell \leq w_{i\ell} \leq U_\ell, keep it unchanged;
  • if wiℓ>Uℓw_{i\ell} > U_\ell, replace it by UℓU_\ell.

Thus, Lℓ≤wiℓwin≤Uℓ. L_\ell \leq w_{i\ell}^{\mathrm{win}} \leq U_\ell.

Using the mean estimation subset ImI_m, define μ̂ℓwin=1nm∑i∈Imwiℓwin\hat\mu_\ell^{\mathrm{win}} = \frac{1}{n_m} \sum_{i\in I_m} w_{i\ell}^{\mathrm{win}}.

The corresponding non-private winsorized scree estimate is

λ̂ℓPMWM,np=nn−1μ̂ℓwin. \hat\lambda_\ell^{\mathrm{PMWM,np}} = \frac{n}{n-1} \hat\mu_\ell^{\mathrm{win}}.

Sensitivity

Because all winsorized observations lie in [Lℓ,Uℓ][L_\ell,U_\ell], the sensitivity of the winsorized mean is

Δℓ(μ)=Uℓ−Lℓnm. \Delta_\ell^{(\mu)} = \frac{U_\ell-L_\ell}{n_m}.

After multiplying by n/(n−1)n/(n-1), the scree sensitivity is Δℓ(λ)=nn−1⋅Uℓ−Lℓnm\Delta_\ell^{(\lambda)} = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot \frac{U_\ell-L_\ell}{n_m}.

DP PMWM scree estimate

The final PMWM scree estimate can be written as

λ̃ℓPMWM=λ̂ℓPMWM,np+ZℓwhereZℓ∼N(0,σℓ2). \widetilde\lambda_\ell^{\mathrm{PMWM}} = \hat\lambda_\ell^{\mathrm{PMWM,np}} + Z_\ell \quad \text{where} \quad Z_\ell \sim N(0,\sigma_\ell^2).

For privacy parameters (ϵM,ℓ,δM,ℓ)(\epsilon_{M,\ell},\delta_{M,\ell}), the noise scale is

σℓ=Δℓ(λ)2log⁡(1.25/δM,ℓ)ϵM,ℓ=(nn−1⋅Uℓ−Lℓnm)2log⁡(1.25/δM,ℓ)ϵM,ℓ. \sigma_\ell = \frac{ \Delta_\ell^{(\lambda)} \sqrt{2\log(1.25/\delta_{M,\ell})} } {\epsilon_{M,\ell}} = \frac{ \left( \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot \frac{U_\ell-L_\ell}{n_m} \right) \sqrt{2\log(1.25/\delta_{M,\ell})} } {\epsilon_{M,\ell}}.

Privacy budget splitting

PMWM uses privacy budget for both private quantile estimation and private mean estimation.

For component ℓ\ell, let the total component-level budget be (ϵℓ,δℓ)(\epsilon_\ell,\delta_\ell).

This can be split as ϵℓ=ϵQ,ℓ+ϵM,ℓ,δℓ=δQ,ℓ+δM,ℓ. \epsilon_\ell = \epsilon_{Q,\ell} + \epsilon_{M,\ell}, \qquad \delta_\ell = \delta_{Q,\ell} + \delta_{M,\ell}.

The quantile budget itself is used to estimate two quantiles, so it can be split again.

ϵQ,ℓ=ϵq1,ℓ+ϵq2,ℓ,δQ,ℓ=δq1,ℓ+δq2,ℓ. \epsilon_{Q,\ell} = \epsilon_{q1,\ell} + \epsilon_{q2,\ell}, \qquad \delta_{Q,\ell} = \delta_{q1,\ell} + \delta_{q2,\ell}.

Parameters

The PMWM scree estimator uses additional parameters for private quantile estimation and winsorization.

  • beta: Log-binning base used in the private quantile estimator.

    It determines the spacing of the geometric search grid and must satisfy β>1\beta > 1.

  • a, b: Lower and upper search bounds supplied to the private quantile. The private lower and upper clipping cutoffs are searched within this range.

  • trim_const, eta: Parameters used to set the practical clipping proportion p=min⁡{max(𝚝𝚛𝚒𝚖_𝚌𝚘𝚗𝚜𝚝nq,η),0.49}. p = \min\left\{ \max\left(\frac{\texttt{trim\_const}}{n_q}, \eta\right), 0.49 \right\}. Here, trim_const / n_q controls the baseline clipping level, while eta gives a lower bound reflecting the expected contamination level.

  • split_mode: Logical value indicating whether the sample is split into two parts.

    If TRUE, one part is used for private quantile estimation and the other part is used for the winsorized mean step. If FALSE, all observations are used in both steps.

  • max_extra_bins: Maximum number of additional log-grid bins searched beyond the largest occupied bin in the private quantile.

Example usage

In dppca, the PMWM-specific parameters can be specified through

scree_pmw_control( beta = beta,a = a,b = b, trim_const = C, eta = eta split_mode = split) \text{scree_pmw_control( beta = beta,a = a,b = b, trim_const = C, eta = eta split_mode = split)} 

dp_scree(
  X,
  k = 3,
  method = "pmwm",
  eps_total = 1,
  delta_total = 1e-6,
  center = TRUE,
  standardize = FALSE,
  control = pmwm_control(
    beta = 1.01,
    a = 0,
    b = 10,
    trim_const = 10,
    eta = 0.01,
    split_mode = TRUE
  )
)

Post-processing

Because of the added privacy noise, the raw DP scree estimates

(λ̃1,…,λ̃k) (\widetilde\lambda_1,\ldots,\widetilde\lambda_k)

may not have the usual scree shape. They may not be decreasing, and some values may be negative.

In ordinary PCA, scree values satisfy

λ1≥λ2≥⋯≥λk≥0. \lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \cdots \ge \lambda_k \ge 0.

Therefore, dppca can apply post-processing to make the DP scree estimates nonnegative and decreasing.

λ̃1mono≥λ̃2mono≥⋯≥λ̃kmono≥0. \widetilde\lambda_1^{\mathrm{mono}} \ge \widetilde\lambda_2^{\mathrm{mono}} \ge \cdots \ge \widetilde\lambda_k^{\mathrm{mono}} \ge 0.

If monotone post-processing is used, the PVE can be computed from the post-processed scree values.

PVẼℓmono=λ̃ℓmono∑j=1kλ̃jmono. \widetilde{\operatorname{PVE}}_\ell^{\mathrm{mono}} = \frac{\widetilde\lambda_\ell^{\mathrm{mono}}} {\sum_{j=1}^k \widetilde\lambda_j^{\mathrm{mono}}}.

This step only modifies the already released DP estimates, so it does not use any additional privacy budget.

Example usage 

dp_scree_plot(
  X,
  k = 3,
  dp_scree_method = "clipped",
  eps_total = 1,
  delta_total = 1e-6,
  control = clipped_control(
    C_clip = 3
  )
)
dp_scree_plot(
  X,
  k = 3,
  dp_scree_method = "all",
  eps_total = 1,
  delta_total = 1e-6,
  center = TRUE,
  standardize = FALSE,
  control = list(
    clipped = clipped_control(
      C_clip = 3
    ),
    pmwm = pmwm_control(
      beta = 1.01,
      a = 0,
      b = 10,
      trim_const = 10,
      eta = 0.01,
      split_mode = TRUE
    ),
    huber = huber_control(
      mu0 = 0,
      eta0 = 1,
      T = 50,
      M = 20,
      k_min_m2 = -40,
      k_max_m2 = 40,
      m2_frac = 1/4
    )
  )
)

References

Myeonghun Yu. Zhao Ren. Wen-Xin Zhou. “Gaussian differentially private robust mean estimation and inference”. Bernoulli 30 (4) 3059 - 3088, November 2024. https://doi.org/10.3150/23-BEJ1706

Kelly Ramsay and Dylan Spicker. (2025). “Improved subsample-and-aggregate via the private modified winsorized mean”. arXiv preprint. https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.14095

Gábor Lugosi. Shahar Mendelson. “Robust multivariate mean estimation: The optimality of trimmed mean.” Ann. Statist. 49 (1) 393 - 410, February 2021. https://doi.org/10.1214/20-AOS1961

On this page

Developed by Yejin Jo, Minwoo Kim.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
